entities.description: The immediate printed source of the U-M encoding. Skeat prints MS Ashmole 44 and TCD MS 213 on facing pages where they overlap, supplies the Ashmole lacuna from Dublin, paraphrases the lost ending editorially, and appends a separate incomplete prose Story of Alexander from the Dublin codex.
entities.label: Walter W. Skeat, The Wars of Alexander (EETS Extra Series 47, 1886)

rights.basis: Published in 1886 and therefore in the public domain in the United States.

entities.description: An undated Kraus Reprint Co. physical reprint whose preliminary statement calls it an unaltered reprint produced with EETS permission. It reproduces the 1886 title page and text but is a distinct print carrier. Because the schema has no general print-carrier type and this is not a new scholarly editing act, catalog_description is the deliberate workaround.
entities.label: Kraus unaltered reprint of Skeat's 1886 Wars of Alexander

rights.basis: The reprint is undated and no item-level license or evidence establishing the legal status of carrier-specific presentation was verified, even though its underlying 1886 editorial text is public domain in the United States.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century northern English paper codex containing the longest and most complete witness of Wars of Alexander as its single text. The work is imperfect at the end and has a lacuna between current fols. 12 and 13.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 44, ff. 1r-97v

notes: One column, 25-34 lines; one scribe in Anglicana.
Catalog date 1440-1460; substantially Durham or possibly south Northumberland dialect with north-west Midland features.

next_steps: Collate the systems against passus, witness, and lacuna boundaries before supplying an intellectual line extent.
openQuestions.question: Can Skeat's conventional 5,677 endpoint, the XML's 5,688 Ashmole L nodes, and the Bodleian catalog's 5,803/approximately-6,300 account be crosswalked without conflating editorial systems?

next_steps: Create a page-and-line concordance using a complete Dublin reproduction and the modern edition.
openQuestions.question: Can Skeat's Dublin line ranges be reconciled with the 2024 Bodleian catalog's modern ranges?

next_steps: Ask Trinity Research Collections for the current catalog record and shelfmark history.
openQuestions.question: What is the current stable item-level Trinity catalog URL and complete physical description for IE TCD MS 213?

next_steps: Collate TCD ff. 70v-72r against the second-edition print witness and Blake 1991.
openQuestions.question: Does direct collation confirm that The Story of Alexander derives from Caxton's second Dicts and Sayings edition, and which exact printing is intended?

next_steps: Request a whole-codex quote and written redistribution terms; identify target ff. 1r-97v.
openQuestions.question: Will Bodleian provide a complete-color or complete-reference facsimile of MS Ashmole 44, and under what repository/publication license and quote?

next_steps: Request the complete codex, identify verse ff. 27r-66v and prose ff. 70v-72r, and obtain item-specific reuse terms.
openQuestions.question: Will TCD provide a complete-color facsimile of IE TCD MS 213, and does an item-specific CC BY 4.0 statement apply to delivered files?

next_steps: Search authority files and EETS correspondence without expanding either display name from inference alone.
openQuestions.question: What exact preferred authority identity should replace Skeat's source-limited Mr. Hessels, and can George Parker be normalized beyond Skeat's printed name?

reviewNotes: Primary transmission path — Wars via Ashmole: artifact:general-corpus:apa1967 -> encoding:umich:APA1967.0001.001 -> edition:skeat:wars-of-alexander:1886 -> witness:bodleian:ashmole-44. Ashmole is the larger base stream; its lacuna is supplied from Dublin.

Primary transmission path — Wars via Dublin: artifact:general-corpus:apa1967 -> encoding:umich:APA1967.0001.001 -> edition:skeat:wars-of-alexander:1886 -> witness:tcd:ms-213. This overlaps Ashmole and is not an additive second half.

Primary transmission path — Story via Dublin: artifact:general-corpus:apa1967 -> encoding:umich:APA1967.0001.001 -> edition:skeat:wars-of-alexander:1886 -> witness:tcd:ms-213. The prose is a separate work at current ff. 70v-72r after intervening account leaves.
Supporting relationship — The U-M raw package supplies both the raw encoding payload and the 506-TIFF printed-edition facsimile payload.
Supporting relationship — The Digital Bodleian object supplies one image of Ashmole 44 fol. 1r only.
Supporting relationship — The Historia de Preliis J3a relation identifies a source tradition, not a direct codex-to-codex transmission claim.
Supporting relationship — The proposed Caxton relation is supported/medium and remains distinct from confirmed primary paths.
Supporting relationship — The Warburg PDF is a facsimile of a Kraus unaltered reprint copied from Skeat 1886; it is not the physical 1886 U-M scan object.
Supporting relationship — Stevenson 1849 and Duggan-Turville-Petre 1989 are supporting editions, not ancestors of apa1967.

lineage.summary: The General Corpus artifact is a pinned copy of U-M encoding APA1967.0001.001, which encodes selected matter from Walter W. Skeat's 1886 edition. That edition contains two distinct historical works: Wars of Alexander (Fragment C), printed through overlapping Ashmole 44 and TCD MS 213 witness streams, and a separate prose Story of Alexander from TCD MS 213. The U-M raw package, its encoding payload, its 506-TIFF printed-volume facsimile, the Kraus unaltered reprint, and the Warburg PDF facsimile are modeled as distinct layers.

catalogSummary.title: The Wars of Alexander: An Alliterative Romance Translated Chiefly from the Historia Alexandri Magni de Preliis

catalogingSubject.label: The Wars of Alexander: Skeat's 1886 edited selection
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from Walter W. Skeat's 1886 EETS Extra Series 47 publication. It includes edition title matter, the verse Wars of Alexander represented through overlapping Ashmole and Dublin witness streams, Skeat's editorial paraphrase of the missing ending, and a separate incomplete prose text editorially headed The Story of Alexander. Omitted front and back matter remains represented by page breaks but is not transcribed. The unit is not one manuscript, not one medieval work, and not two sequential halves of one verse text.

contentParts.label: Wars of Alexander (Alexander Fragment C)

locators.value: pp. 1-276 for the medieval verse; Skeat's ending paraphrase continues through p. 279

notes: The XML contains 27 passus containers, 40 verse-group containers, and 8,509 serialized L elements: 5,688 in the Ashmole stream and 2,821 in the Dublin stream, with 24 parallel-version containers.
The parallel streams are not additive, so those encoding counts are not stored as intellectual verse-group or line extent.
The Ashmole stream combines 2,735 unwrapped L elements with 2,953 L elements inside Ashmole version containers.
Skeat's final conventional number 5,677 and modern catalog numbering remain unreconciled.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Middle English Alexander romance in unrhymed alliterative verse, incomplete at its end and represented in Skeat by two overlapping manuscript witnesses.

contentParts.label: The Story of Alexander
notes: The XML has six direct narrative P elements; its separate ARGUMENT/P is editorial and excluded from the historical prose work.
Accounts and miscellanea at current ff. 67r-70r separate this prose from the verse witness.
Khalaf reports that scholars consider ff. 70v-72r copied from Caxton's second Dicts and Sayings edition; the relation remains supported/medium pending direct collation.
contentParts.summary: A separate, incomplete Middle English prose epitome copied from IE TCD MS 213 and placed after intervening account leaves in that codex.

notes: Keep this distinct from Ashmole's 1440-1460 copying date and the late-fifteenth-century Dublin witness.
This is a witness-attestation range, not a composition date. If the proposed Caxton-source relation is confirmed, copying must postdate the second edition circa 1480.

dateStatements.display: The source selection was published in 1886

extent.description: Two historical works are represented: one verse work and one prose work. The encoding has 40 verse-group containers and 8,509 serialized L elements, but these include overlapping Ashmole and Dublin witness streams and therefore are not an intellectual poem extent. Verse-group and verse-line totals remain null.

formStatements.description: The edition-level selection juxtaposes one verse work and one separate prose work; neither historical work is internally mixed-form.
Unrhymed alliterative long-line verse, AA/AX with variation.

genres.label: Wisdom literature
notes: The classification reflects the proposed relationship to Caxton's Dicts and Sayings and remains tentative pending direct source collation.

scope.description: Edition headings, argument, and ending paraphrase

notes: Edition title matter at TIFF 1-2 is paratext and contributes no historical work count.
Skeat's editorial paraphrase of the missing ending at printed pp. 276-279, TIFF 298-301, and 14 P elements nested in the final verse L/NOTE1 is not counted as medieval verse.
Skeat's ARGUMENT/P before The Story of Alexander at printed p. 279 is editorial introduction and is excluded from the historical prose work.
Omitted front and back matter survives only as encoding placeholders, chiefly front PB references to TIFF 3-21 and back PB references to TIFF 306-506.
U-M's 2006 digital-production date is recorded on the encoding and facsimile entities in the paired lineage manifest because the work-metadata date vocabulary has no digital-production type.
Witness copying dates, dialects, locations, and image terms are not promoted to the abstract historical works.

next_steps: Collate the three numbering systems against passus, witness, and lacuna boundaries before supplying an intellectual line extent.

summaries.value: Skeat's edition-level selection brings together two distinct Middle English Alexander works: an incomplete alliterative romance printed from overlapping Ashmole and Dublin witness streams, and a separate incomplete prose epitome from the Dublin codex. The encoded selection also retains editorial title matter, Skeat's prose account of the lost verse ending, and his argument before the prose text.

The verse work narrates Alexander's conquests, marvels, kingship, treachery, and death in unrhymed alliterative long lines. Skeat prints Ashmole and Dublin in parallel where they overlap and uses Dublin to supply Ashmole's lacuna; the streams are alternative witnesses, not additive poem segments.

The separate prose Story of Alexander is incomplete at its end and survives in TCD MS 213 after leaves of accounts and miscellanea. Scholarship reports a possible source in Caxton's second edition of the Dicts and Sayings, but direct collation remains outstanding.

notes: This is an edition-level wrapper title, not a title shared by both historical works.
titles.supplied_by: Skeat's 1886 title page, shortened before its edition and witness statement

The encoded selection also retains editorial title matter, Skeat's prose account of the lost verse ending, and his argument before the prose text.
